title: General — When to Use a Multiple-Address Letter
plain_language_rule: A multiple-address letter is used when identical correspondence (same subject and content) must be sent to more than one activity or individual within the Department of the Navy. It may also be used for commands of other services, other Government agencies, and the general public when conditions permit. A multiple-address letter follows the standard letter format from Chapter 7, with the modifications described in this chapter. Do not use a multiple-address letter when: (1) separate letters with different enclosures per addressee would be prepared anyway, (2) addressees are at different levels in the chain of command (e.g., some To, some Via), or (3) the letter requires an individual signature of the originator for each addressee.
renderer_impact: Determines whether the correspondence uses a single-address or multiple-address format. When a multiple-address letter is selected, the renderer must apply Chapter 8 modifications (addressing, distribution, copies, assembly) on top of the Chapter 7 standard letter base format.
notes: This is a scope/selection rule defining when to use (or not use) a multiple-address letter. It is primarily a procedural decision for the originator — the rule says 'when the originator determines.' The three exclusion criteria involve content-level judgments (different enclosures per addressee, command chain position, individual signature requirements) that are not amenable to automated layout validation. The rule explicitly defers to Chapter 7 for the base letter format; Chapter 8 only describes modifications. The phrase 'when military and Government standards make its use feasible' for non-DON addressees is an additional human judgment gate.
